title: Parameters
lesson:
When people say a model is "7B" or "70B" or "a trillion", that B means billion, and the thing being counted is parameters. A parameter is just a single number the model learned during training. That's it. A model is, at heart, a gigantic pile of these numbers plus some fixed math that mixes them together. The numbers are what make one model smart and another model dumb.

The mixing-desk analogy

Picture a recording studio mixing desk covered in thousands of sliders and knobs. The wiring of the desk never changes — that's the model's architecture (the fixed layout, mostly a Transformer). But the position of every slider decides what sound comes out. Training is the long process of nudging every slider, a hair at a time, until the output sounds right. Each slider position is one parameter. A 7-billion-parameter model is a desk with 7 billion sliders, all set to just the right spot. When you download a model, you are downloading the slider positions, not the songs.

Most parameters are weights — numbers that say how strongly one piece of information should influence another. A few are biases — small nudges added on top, like a thumb resting on a scale. Together, weights and biases are stored in big grids of numbers called matrices (think spreadsheets full of decimals). The model's clever behaviour — grammar, facts, reasoning — isn't written down anywhere as rules. It is baked into these millions or billions of slider positions.

How they actually get used

Every time you send a prompt, the model multiplies your input by these weight matrices, layer after layer, to predict the next token. The attention mechanism and the rest of the network are just structured ways of applying parameters. So a parameter is not abstract trivia — it is the literal substance doing the thinking on every single word.

A second analogy: the staffed restaurant

Imagine hiring for a restaurant. The architecture is the org chart — who reports to whom, how the kitchen is laid out. The parameters are the skills and instincts each cook has built up over years. You can't open a cook's head and read a rule that says "add salt here"; their judgement is spread across countless tiny learned habits. More cooks (more parameters) can handle a more elaborate menu — but only if you trained them well. A huge, untrained staff is just an expensive, confused kitchen.

Why parameter count matters (and where it lies to you)

Roughly speaking, more parameters means more capacity — more room to store patterns and nuance. That's why a 70B model usually reasons better than a 7B one. But count alone is a noisy signal:

  • Training quality beats raw size. A well-trained 8B model in 2026 can beat a sloppily trained 70B from a few years back. The sliders have to be set well, not just exist.
  • Architecture changes the meaning. A Mixture-of-Experts model might have 100B parameters but only "switch on" 12B for any given token — so it's cheaper to run than its sticker number suggests.
  • Small can be plenty. For focused jobs, a tidy small language model often wins on speed and cost.

The hard practical fact: memory

Parameters are the single biggest reason models are expensive to run. Each parameter is a number that must be stored in VRAM (the fast memory on a GPU). The size depends on the precision — how many bytes each number uses:

7,000,000,000 params x 2 bytes (16-bit) = ~14 GB
7,000,000,000 params x 1 byte  (8-bit)  = ~7 GB
7,000,000,000 params x 0.5 byte (4-bit) = ~3.5 GB

That rule of thumb — params x bytes-per-param — tells you instantly whether a model fits on your hardware. Shrinking the bytes-per-param is exactly what quantization does, and it's why a 7B model can run on a laptop while a 70B one needs a serious GPU. You can also check a count in code without downloading the whole weights:

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B")
total = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f"{total:,} parameters")   # 1,235,814,400

Finally, parameters connect directly to fine-tuning. Full fine-tuning nudges all of them — costly. Methods like LoRA freeze the billions and train a tiny add-on instead. Understanding that a model is its parameters is the key that makes the rest of this course click.
mentalModel: A giant studio mixing desk with billions of sliders. The wiring (architecture) is fixed; training sets every slider to just the right spot. The model isn't rules — it's those slider positions, and downloading a model means downloading the positions.
mistakes: Treating parameter count as a quality score. Bigger is not automatically better. A well-trained smaller model often beats a poorly trained larger one. Count is capacity, not skill — the sliders still have to be set well.

Confusing total parameters with active parameters. In a Mixture-of-Experts model, only a slice of the parameters fire per token. The headline number overstates how much compute and memory each request actually uses.

Forgetting that precision changes the memory math. '7B params' isn't a fixed size in gigabytes. At 16-bit it's ~14 GB; at 4-bit it's ~3.5 GB. Always ask 'how many bytes per parameter?' before guessing if it fits.

Mixing up parameters with the context window. Parameters are the learned weights (fixed after training). The context window is how much text the model can read at once. A bigger model does not automatically read more text.

Assuming parameters store facts like a database. Knowledge is smeared across billions of numbers, not filed in lookup rows. That's why models can blur or invent facts — and why RAG exists to feed them real documents instead.

Thinking you must train all parameters to customise a model. Full fine-tuning touches every weight and is expensive. LoRA and friends freeze the lot and train a tiny fraction, getting most of the benefit for a fraction of the cost.
exercise:
Pick three open models of different sizes — say a 1B, an 8B, and a 70B (you can read their cards on Hugging Face without downloading). For each, use the params x bytes-per-param rule of thumb to estimate the VRAM needed at 16-bit precision, then again at 4-bit. Write the six numbers in a little table and mark which ones would fit on a typical 24 GB consumer GPU.

Stretch goal: Actually load the 1B model with the transformers snippet above, print its real parameter count, and compare your 16-bit memory estimate to the real GPU memory it uses when loaded. Why might the real number be a bit higher? (Hint: the weights aren't the only thing living in VRAM.)
solution: Worked answer: Using params x bytes: at 16-bit (2 bytes) the models need roughly 2 GB, 16 GB, and 140 GB; at 4-bit (0.5 byte) roughly 0.5 GB, 4 GB, and 35 GB. On a 24 GB GPU: the 1B fits easily either way, the 8B fits at both precisions, the 70B fits at neither raw — you'd need 4-bit and still be over 24 GB, so it needs multiple GPUs or heavier compression. The real loaded number is higher than the weight estimate because VRAM also holds activations, the KV cache for your prompt, and framework overhead — the weights are the biggest slice, but never the only one.
summary: Parameters are the billions of learned numbers (weights and biases) that are the model — fixed math mixes them to predict the next token. More parameters means more capacity, but training quality, architecture (e.g. MoE), and precision matter just as much. The simplest power tool: VRAM needed ≈ params x bytes-per-parameter.

keyTerms.def: A single number the model learned during training. A model is mostly just a huge collection of these numbers.
The most common kind of parameter: a number saying how strongly one piece of information influences another.
A small parameter added on top of a calculation, like a thumb on a scale, to shift the result a little.
The fixed wiring of the model — how the layers are arranged. It doesn't change during training; only the parameters do.
How much pattern and nuance a model can store. Roughly grows with parameter count, but isn't the same as actual skill.
How many bytes each parameter uses (e.g. 16-bit, 8-bit, 4-bit). Fewer bytes means a smaller, faster model that needs less memory.
In a Mixture-of-Experts model, the subset of parameters that actually run for a given token — usually far fewer than the total.
Parameters that are locked and not updated. Methods like LoRA freeze the whole base model and train a tiny add-on instead.
